label: Localized nail polish contact rash without infection or anaphylaxis
summary: Your symptoms fit a localized skin reaction from nail polish or nail product exposure.
found: Your clinician diagnosed a localized nail polish contact skin reaction. We did not find anaphylaxis, mucosal involvement, cellulitis, abscess, felon, or another rash emergency today.
home: Avoid the suspected trigger if it is known.
Wash gently with mild soap and water, and avoid scrubbing the rash.
Use cool compresses, moisturizers, or coverings only as your clinician recommended.
meds: Use creams, ointments, itch medicine, or allergy medicine only if prescribed or recommended.
Do not use leftover steroid, antibiotic, or allergy medicine unless your clinician told you to.
return: Trouble breathing, throat tightness, swelling of the lips or tongue, fainting, or a bodywide reaction.
Fever, spreading redness, warmth, pus, red streaks, rapidly worsening rash, skin peeling, mouth sores, worsening finger pain, abscess, or felon symptoms.
Symptoms that worsen or do not improve as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, urgent care, dermatology, allergy, hand clinic, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of localized nail polish or nail product contact skin reaction.

No anaphylaxis, systemic allergic reaction, mucosal involvement, cellulitis, abscess, felon/tenosynovitis concern, severe medication reaction, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Anaphylaxis, airway symptoms, hypotension, systemic allergic reaction, mucosal lesions, severe cutaneous adverse reaction, cellulitis, abscess, felon/tenosynovitis concern, rapidly progressive rash, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed dermatology/allergy/wound plan.

terms: clinician diagnosis localized nail polish contact rash
discharge diagnosis localized nail polish contact rash
localized nail polish contact rash diagnosed

high_confidence_terms: clinician diagnosis localized nail polish contact rash
localized nail polish contact rash diagnosed
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports contact dermatitis framing from irritant or allergic exposures, trigger avoidance, gentle skin care, clinician-directed topical or allergy treatment, and escalation for severe allergic, infectious, or mucosal red flags. This expansion is limited to clinician-diagnosed localized nail polish contact skin reaction with anaphylaxis, infection, abscess/felon, mucosal, pregnancy/pediatric, severe-rash, and specialist-directed pathways absent.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis of localized nail polish contact skin reaction
localized reaction only
no anaphylaxis
no infection, abscess/felon, mucosal, or severe-rash red flags

found: Your clinician diagnosed a localized topical antibiotic contact skin reaction. We did not find anaphylaxis, systemic allergic reaction, skin infection, mucosal involvement, severe medication reaction, or another rash emergency today.
home: Stop using the suspected trigger unless your clinician told you otherwise.
Wash gently with mild soap and water, and avoid scrubbing the rash.
Use cool compresses, moisturizers, or coverings only as your clinician recommended.
meds: Use creams, ointments, itch medicine, or allergy medicine only if prescribed or recommended.
Do not use leftover steroid, antibiotic, or allergy medicine unless your clinician told you to.
return: Trouble breathing, throat tightness, swelling of the lips or tongue, fainting, or a bodywide reaction.
Fever, spreading redness, warmth, pus, red streaks, rapidly worsening rash, skin peeling, mouth sores, or facial swelling.
Symptoms that worsen or do not improve as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, urgent care, dermatology, allergy, wound care, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of localized topical antibiotic contact skin reaction.
No anaphylaxis, systemic allergic reaction, mucosal involvement, skin infection, severe medication reaction, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Anaphylaxis, airway symptoms, hypotension, systemic allergic reaction, mucosal lesions, severe cutaneous adverse reaction, cellulitis or wound infection concern, rapidly progressive rash, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed dermatology/allergy/wound plan.

label: Dry cracked elbows without infection or systemic skin-disease red flags
summary: Your symptoms fit dry, cracked skin on the elbows.
found: Your clinician diagnosed dry, cracked elbow skin. We did not find skin infection, open deep wound, severe rash, systemic illness, or another skin emergency today.
home: Use gentle skin care and avoid harsh soaps, scrubbing, or triggers that worsen the dryness.
Use moisturizer, protective covering, or activity limits only as your clinician recommended.
Avoid picking or scratching the cracked skin.
meds: Use creams, ointments, itch medicine, or other skin medicine only if prescribed or recommended.
Do not use leftover steroid, antibiotic, or antifungal medicine unless your clinician told you to.
return: Fever, spreading redness, warmth, pus, red streaks, rapidly worsening pain, or worsening swelling.
Skin peeling, mouth sores, a bodywide rash, or feeling very ill.
Symptoms that worsen or do not improve as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, urgent care, dermatology, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of dry, cracked elbow skin or elbow xerosis.
No skin infection, deep open wound, severe cutaneous reaction, systemic illness, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Skin infection, cellulitis, abscess, deep open wound, severe cutaneous adverse reaction, skin peeling or sloughing, mucosal lesions, systemic illness, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed dermatology/wound plan.
must_not_miss: Systemic skin disease flare requiring separate care.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports dry-skin self-care with gentle skin care, moisturizers, avoiding irritants, and escalation for infection, severe rash, or systemic symptoms. This expansion is limited to clinician-diagnosed dry cracked elbows with infection, deep wound, severe-rash, systemic, pregnancy/pediatric, immunocompromised, and specialist-directed pathways absent.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis of dry cracked elbows or elbow xerosis
localized dry cracked skin
no infection or deep open wound
no systemic or mucosal red flags

label: Minor superficial cut without deep wound or infection red flags
summary: Your symptoms fit a minor superficial cut.
found: Your clinician diagnosed a minor superficial cut. We did not find tendon, nerve, blood-vessel, joint, bone, retained foreign-body, bite, or infection concern today.
home: Keep the wound clean and dry as your clinician instructed.
Change dressings, use soap and water, and protect the area only as your clinician recommended.
Avoid soaking, picking, or reopening the cut.
meds: Use pain medicine only if prescribed or recommended, and follow the label.
Use antibiotic ointment, tetanus care, or other wound treatment only if your clinician recommended it.
return: Fever, spreading redness, warmth, pus, red streaks, worsening swelling, or worsening pain.
Numbness, weakness, trouble moving the area, bleeding that will not stop, or concern that something is still in the wound.
The wound opens, worsens, or does not improve as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, urgent care, wound care, hand clinic, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of minor superficial cut not requiring repair.

No deep structure injury, tendon/nerve/blood-vessel injury, joint violation, open fracture, retained foreign body, bite wound, dirty wound requiring separate pathway, infection, diabetic-foot risk, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Repair-needed laceration, tendon/nerve/blood-vessel injury, joint violation, open fracture, retained foreign body, bite wound, dirty wound, infection concern, neurovascular compromise, severe pain, delayed high-risk presentation, diabetic foot, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed wound plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports basic cuts and wound-care self-care, keeping wounds clean, clinician-directed tetanus/ointment decisions, and escalation for infection, bleeding, retained foreign body, or deep-structure concern. This expansion is limited to clinician-diagnosed minor superficial cuts that do not need repair and have no deep structure, foreign-body, bite, dirty wound, infection, diabetic-foot, pregnancy/pediatric, or specialist-directed pathway.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis of minor superficial cut
no repair needed
no deep structure injury or retained foreign body
no infection
intact neurovascular exam
